en.extract:
The brown skua (Stercorarius antarcticus), also known as the Antarctic skua, Subantarctic skua, southern great skua, southern skua, or hākoakoa (Māori), is a large seabird that breeds in the subantarctic and Antarctic zones and moves farther north when not breeding. Its taxonomy is highly complex and a matter of dispute, with some splitting it into two or three species: Falkland skua (S. antarcticus), Tristan skua (S. hamiltoni), and subantarctic skua (S. lönnbergi). To further confuse, it hybridizes with both the south polar and Chilean skuas, and the entire group has been considered to be a subspecies of the great skua, a species otherwise restricted to the Northern Hemisphere.


Description
This is the heaviest species of skua and rivals the largest gulls, the great black-backed gull and glaucous gull, as the heaviest species in the shorebird order although not as large in length or wingspan. It is 52–64 cm (20–25 in) in length, 126–160 cm (50–63 in) in wingspan and has a body mass of 1.2–2.18 kg (2.6–4.8 lb). S. a. hamiltoni measured on Gough Island, weighed an average of 1.43 kg (3.2 lb) in 9 males and 1.65 kg (3.6 lb) in 9 females. S. a. lonnbergi measured in the Chatham Islands weighed an average of 1.73 kg (3.8 lb) in 30 males and an average of 1.93 kg (4.3 lb) in 32 females. The latter is one of the highest colony mean body mass for any living species of shorebird.
A study in 2016 reported that brown skuas can identify individual human beings, possibly indicating high cognitive abilities.
Brown skuas have been noted for sometimes bonding with humans who live for extended periods in Antarctica, such as the Eastern Orthodox clergymen at Trinity Church, and engaging in playful or apparently mischievous behavior with them.


Taxonomy
There are three accepted subspecies:

S. antarcticus lonnbergi. Range: subantarctic.
S. antarcticus antarcticus. Range: Falkland Islands.
S. antarctis hamiltoni. Range: Tristan da Cunha.


Ecology


Diet
It feeds on fish (often via kleptoparasitism), penguin chicks and other seabirds, small mammals, eggs and carrion.


References


Further reading
Harrison, Peter (1996). Seabirds - an identification guide. Christopher Helm: A & C Black, London. ISBN 0-7136-3510-X.
Heather, Barrie D; Robertson, Hugh A & Onley, Derek (2000). The field guide to the birds of New Zealand. Viking: Printing Press. ISBN 0-670-89370-6.


External links
Brown skua, subantarctic skua - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds

''S. a. hamiltoni'' – (Hagen, 1952)
''S. a. lonnbergi'' – ([[Gregory Mathews|Mathews]], 1912) | range_map = Catharacta antarctica map.svg }} The '''brown skua''' ('''''Stercorarius antarcticus'''''), also known as the '''Antarctic skua''', '''Subantarctic skua''', '''southern great skua''', '''southern skua''', or '''hākoakoa''' ([[Māori language|Māori]]), is a large [[seabird]] that breeds in the [[subantarctic]] and [[Antarctic]] zones and moves farther north when not breeding. Its [[Taxonomy (biology)|taxonomy]] is highly complex and a matter of dispute, with some [[lumpers and splitters|splitting]] it into two or three species: Falkland skua (''S. antarcticus''), Tristan skua (''S. hamiltoni''), and subantarctic skua (''S. lönnbergi''). To further confuse, it [[Hybrid (biology)|hybridizes]] with both the [[south polar skua|south polar]] and [[Chilean skua|Chilean]] skuas, and the entire group has been considered to be a [[subspecies]] of the [[great skua]], a species otherwise restricted to the [[Northern Hemisphere]]. ==Description== [[File:Brown Skua eyeing a King Penguin carcass (5724592724).jpg|thumb|200px|left|Brown skua eyeing a [[king penguin]] carcass]]This is the heaviest species of [[skua]] and rivals the largest [[gull]]s, the [[great black-backed gull]] and [[glaucous gull]], as the heaviest species in the [[Charadriiformes|shorebird order]] although not as large in length or wingspan.{{Cite web |title=Stercorarius antarcticus lonnbergi — Brown Skua (Lonnberg's), Southern Great Skua |url=http://www.environment.gov.au/cgi-bin/sprat/public/publicspecies.pl?taxon_id=85040 |website=Species Profile and Threats Database Australian Government}} It is {{convert|52|-|64|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length, {{convert|126|-|160|cm|in|abbr=on}} in wingspan and has a body mass of {{convert|1.2|-|2.18|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.[http://www.lynxeds.com/hbw/species-accounts/hbw-3-species-accounts-brown-skua-catharacta-antarctica HBW 3 - Species accounts: Brown Skua] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120205230613/http://www.lynxeds.com/hbw/species-accounts/hbw-3-species-accounts-brown-skua-catharacta-antarctica |date=2012-02-05 }} (2011).''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses'' by John B. Dunning Jr. (Editor). CRC Press (1992), {{ISBN|978-0-8493-4258-5}}. ''S. a. hamiltoni'' measured on [[Gough Island]], weighed an average of {{convert|1.43|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in 9 males and {{convert|1.65|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in 9 females. ''S. a. lonnbergi'' measured in the [[Chatham Islands]] weighed an average of {{convert|1.73|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in 30 males and an average of {{convert|1.93|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in 32 females. The latter is one of the highest colony mean body mass for any living species of shorebird.''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses, 2nd Edition'' by John B. Dunning Jr. (Editor). CRC Press (2008), {{ISBN|978-1-4200-6444-5}}. A study in 2016 reported that brown skuas can identify individual human beings, possibly indicating high cognitive abilities.{{cite journal|author=Lee, W.Y.|author2=Han, Y.D.|author3=Jablonski, P.G.|author4=Jung, J.W.|author5=Kim, J.H.|name-list-style=amp|year=2016|pages=1–5|title=Antarctic skuas recognize individual humans|journal=Animal Cognition|volume=19|issue=4|doi=10.1007/s10071-016-0970-9|pmid=26939544|s2cid=254131457 }} Brown skuas have been noted for sometimes bonding with humans who live for extended periods in Antarctica, such as the [[Eastern Orthodox]] clergymen at [[Trinity Church (Antarctica)|Trinity Church]], and engaging in playful or apparently mischievous behavior with them.{{Cite web|last=Mihailova|first=Natalya|date=March 6, 2015|title=Russian priest feels closer to God in serenity of Antarctica|url=https://www.pravmir.com/russian-priest-feels-closer-to-god-in-serenity-of-antarctica/|url-status=live|archive-url=https://archive.today/20201130025334/https://www.pravmir.com/russian-priest-feels-closer-to-god-in-serenity-of-antarctica/|archive-date=November 30, 2020|access-date=November 28, 2020|website=Pravmir}} == Taxonomy == There are three accepted subspecies:{{Cite journal |last1=Furness |first1=Robert W. |last2=Boesman |first2=Peter F. D. |last3=Garcia |first3=Ernest |date=2020 |title=Brown Skua (Stercorarius antarcticus), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/brnsku3/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.brnsku3.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }} * ''S. antarcticus lonnbergi.'' Range: subantarctic. * ''S. antarcticus antarcticus.'' Range: Falkland Islands. * ''S. antarctis hamiltoni.'' Range: [[Tristan da Cunha]]. == Ecology == === Diet === It feeds on [[fish]] (often via [[kleptoparasitism]]), penguin chicks and other [[seabird]]s, small [[mammal]]s, eggs and [[carrion]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Further reading== * {{cite book | author=Harrison, Peter | title=Seabirds - an identification guide | location=Christopher Helm | publisher=A & C Black, London | year=1996 | isbn=0-7136-3510-X}} * {{cite book |author1=Heather, Barrie D |author2=Robertson, Hugh A |author3=Onley, Derek |name-list-style=amp| title=The field guide to the birds of New Zealand | location=Viking | publisher=Printing Press | year=2000 | isbn=0-670-89370-6}} ==External links== * Brown skua, subantarctic skua - [http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/310.pdf Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds] {{Commons category|Stercorarius antarcticus}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q18987}} [[Category:Skuas|brown skua]] [[Category:Birds of Antarctica]] [[Category:Birds of New Zealand]] [[Category:Birds of the Falkland Islands]] [[Category:Birds of Southern Africa]] [[Category:Birds of islands of the Atlantic Ocean]] [[Category:Fauna of the Prince Edward Islands]] [[Category:Fauna of Heard Island and McDonald Islands]] [[Category:Birds of subantarctic islands]] [[Category:Birds described in 1831|brown skua]] [[Category:Taxa named by René Lesson]] [[Category:Taxobox binomials not recognized by IUCN]] [[Category:Fauna of Macquarie Island]]

zh.extract:
棕色贼鸥（學名：Stercorarius antarcticus）是一种大型海鸟，詞類物種主要在亚南极和南极地区繁殖。它以魚、企鹅雏鸟和其他海鳥、小型哺乳动物、鸡蛋和腐肉为食。根據2016年的一项研究报告表示，棕色贼鸥可能具有较高的认知能力。
